title: The Sentence Machine
point: A sentence needs a who-or-what and a what-happened idea.
captions: Every complete sentence has a subject and a predicate.
The subject is who or what the sentence is about.
The predicate tells what the subject does or is.
A complete thought feels finished when you read it.
script: Welcome to English Grammar. Today we are building sentence power, one clear idea at a time. First, think of a sentence as a little machine. A machine needs parts that work together. A sentence needs a subject, a predicate, and a complete thought. The subject is the who or what. In the sentence, The curious dog chased the red ball, the subject is the curious dog. That is who the sentence is about. The predicate tells what happened. Chased the red ball is the predicate. It gives the action and finishes the idea. Now try a smaller one: Maya reads a comic. Maya is the subject. Reads a comic is the predicate. If I only say, reads a comic, your brain asks, who reads a comic? If I only say Maya, your brain asks, what about Maya? A strong sentence answers both questions. Here is your trick: ask, who or what is this about? Then ask, what happened or what is true? When both answers are there, you probably have a complete sentence.

title: Nouns And Pronouns
point: Nouns name things. Pronouns replace nouns so writing is smoother.
captions: Nouns name people, places, things, and ideas.
Pronouns stand in for nouns.
Good pronouns make writing smoother.
The reader must still know who or what you mean.
script: Now we meet nouns and pronouns. A noun names a person, place, thing, or idea. Teacher is a person. Garden is a place. Bicycle is a thing. Courage is an idea. Nouns are the labels that help a reader know exactly what we are talking about. But if we repeat the same noun too many times, the writing starts to sound clunky. Listen to this: Sofia packed Sofia's bag because Sofia was going to Sofia's lesson. That is clear, but it is not smooth. Pronouns can help. A pronoun stands in for a noun. She, he, they, it, we, and you are pronouns. Now listen again: Sofia packed her bag because she was going to her lesson. Much smoother. The important rule is that the reader must still know who the pronoun means. If two girls are in the sentence and I say she, the reader may get confused. So use nouns for clarity, and pronouns for flow. Quick check: The players cheered after the players won. We can improve it to: The players cheered after they won.

title: Verbs And Tense
point: Verbs show action or being. Tense tells when it happens.
captions: Verbs are doing or being words.
Tense tells time: past, present, or future.
Some past-tense verbs are irregular.
Choose the tense that matches the sentence time.
script: Verbs are the engine words. They show action or being. Jump, think, build, laugh, is, were, and has are all verbs. A sentence without a verb usually feels unfinished. Now verbs also carry time. That time is called tense. Past tense means it happened before. Present tense means it is happening now, or it is generally true. Future tense means it will happen later. Watch the timeline on the board. Yesterday I walked. Today I walk. Tomorrow I will walk. The verb changes because the time changes. Many past-tense verbs add ed, like walked, jumped, or painted. But English likes a surprise. Some verbs are irregular. We say ran, not runned. We say ate, not eated. We say saw, not seed. When you edit your writing, check that the verb time matches the sentence. If the story happened yesterday, most verbs should sound like yesterday. Quick check: change I see a bird to past tense. The answer is I saw a bird. Saw is the past tense of see.

title: Adjectives And Adverbs
point: Adjectives describe nouns. Adverbs describe verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs.
captions: Adjectives describe nouns.
Adverbs often describe verbs.
Ask what kind, which one, how many, how, when, or where.
Use detail that helps the reader picture the idea.
script: Adjectives and adverbs are detail tools. They help the reader picture the idea more clearly. An adjective describes a noun. If I say kite, you can picture a kite. If I say bright blue kite, the picture becomes stronger. Bright and blue are adjectives because they describe the noun kite. Adjectives often answer what kind, which one, or how many. Now adverbs usually describe verbs. If I say the turtle moved, you know the action. If I say the turtle moved slowly, you know how it moved. Slowly is an adverb. Adverbs can answer how, when, where, or how much. Here is a sentence: The brave girl spoke clearly. Brave describes the noun girl, so brave is an adjective. Clearly describes the verb spoke, so clearly is an adverb. One warning: do not add detail just to decorate. Add detail when it helps meaning. The tiny silver key opened the old gate is useful detail. The very extremely amazingly shiny tiny silver key can become too heavy. Good writers choose details like careful artists.

title: Prepositions
point: Prepositions show position, direction, time, or relationship.
captions: Prepositions are relationship words.
They often begin prepositional phrases.
One preposition can change the whole picture.
Prepositional phrases add detail, but they are not the main verb.
script: Prepositions are small words with a big job. They show relationships. They can show position, direction, time, or connection. Listen to these: on, under, beside, between, after, before, during, into, across, and near. If I say the book is on the desk, you know where it is. If I say the book is under the desk, the picture changes completely. A preposition often begins a prepositional phrase. Under the table is a phrase. After lunch is a phrase. Near the tall tree is a phrase. These phrases add helpful detail. But be careful: a prepositional phrase is usually not the main subject or the main verb. In the sentence, The cat slept under the chair, the subject is cat and the verb is slept. Under the chair tells where the cat slept. Here is your quick test. If a word tells where, when, or how two things are related, it may be a preposition. Prepositions are like tiny signposts. They tell the reader where to look.

title: Conjunctions
point: Conjunctions connect words, phrases, and clauses.
captions: Conjunctions are joining words.
And adds. But contrasts. Or offers a choice. So gives a result.
A comma often comes before a conjunction joining two full sentences.
Choose the connector that matches the relationship.
script: Conjunctions are joining words. They connect words, phrases, or complete ideas. A useful set to remember is for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so. Many students remember them as fanboys. And adds something. I packed a pencil and a ruler. But shows contrast. I wanted to play, but it rained. Or gives a choice. We can read or draw. So shows a result. I was hungry, so I made toast. The clever part is choosing the connector that matches the relationship between ideas. If two ideas go together, and may work. If one idea pushes against the other, but may work. If one idea causes the next, so may work. When you join two complete sentences with a conjunction, you often put a comma before the conjunction. I finished my homework, so I played outside. Both sides could stand as sentences. Quick check: I wore a coat, because it was cold. Because explains the reason. Conjunctions are bridges. Good bridges make the sentence easy to cross.

title: Punctuation And Capitals
point: Punctuation and capital letters help readers hear the sentence correctly.
captions: Capital letters and punctuation are reading signals.
Capitals start sentences and mark names, places, days, and months.
End marks tell the reader how the sentence sounds.
Commas organise lists and joined ideas.
script: Punctuation and capitals are reading signals. They tell the reader where ideas start, where they stop, and how they should sound. Start every sentence with a capital letter. Capitalize names, places, days, months, and important titles. We write Monday with a capital M. We write Leo with a capital L. End marks matter too. A period ends a telling sentence. The door is open. A question mark ends a question. Where is my pencil? An exclamation mark shows strong feeling. That was amazing! Commas help organise sentences. They separate items in a list: I packed a pen, a snack, and a hat. They also often appear before a conjunction that joins two complete sentences. I wanted to keep reading, but the bell rang. Now watch the repair: on monday leo asked where is my pencil. It should become: On Monday, Leo asked, Where is my pencil? Better punctuation makes the sentence easier to hear inside your head. It is not decoration. It is the reader's road map.

title: Paragraph Repair
point: Use grammar choices to make a paragraph clearer.
captions: Editing is grammar in action.
Check each sentence for a subject and predicate.
Replace repeated nouns when the meaning stays clear.
Use detail and punctuation to guide the reader.
script: Now we use grammar like editors. Editing is not just hunting for mistakes. Editing means making the writing clearer for a reader. First, check every sentence. Does it have a subject? Does it have a predicate? If a sentence says, Ran through the park, it needs a subject. Who ran? Maya ran through the park. Next, look for repeated nouns. Maya opened Maya's notebook because Maya wanted Maya's story to be neat. We can repair it: Maya opened her notebook because she wanted her story to be neat. Then check details. Are adjectives and adverbs helping, or just crowding the sentence? The small red kite rose quickly gives a useful picture. Finally, check capitals, commas, and end marks. A paragraph does not need to be fancy to be strong. It needs to be clear, complete, and easy to follow. Here is the editor's order: sentence parts first, word choices second, punctuation third. When you edit in that order, you fix the meaning before you polish the surface.

title: Grammar Power-Up Quiz
point: Review the course with quick examples and choices.
captions: Final round: identify the job each word is doing.
Find nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs.
Use prepositions and conjunctions to explain relationships.
Grammar helps your ideas travel clearly.
script: Final round. We are going to test the big grammar ideas quickly. Look at this sentence: The tiny robot danced happily beside the desk. First, find the noun. Robot is a noun because it names a thing. Desk is also a noun. Now find the verb. Danced is the verb because it shows the action. Find the adjective. Tiny describes robot, so tiny is an adjective. Find the adverb. Happily tells how the robot danced, so happily is an adverb. Find the prepositional phrase. Beside the desk tells where the robot danced. Beside is the preposition. Now add a conjunction: The tiny robot danced happily beside the desk, and the class clapped. And joins two related ideas. Last check: does the sentence start with a capital and end with the right punctuation? Yes. You now have a grammar toolkit: sentence, noun, pronoun, verb, tense, adjective, adverb, preposition, conjunction, capital letters, and punctuation. Grammar is not about sounding fancy. Grammar helps your ideas travel clearly from your mind to your reader.

title: Clauses And Phrases
point: A clause has a subject and verb. A phrase adds detail but cannot stand alone.
captions: A clause has a subject and verb. A phrase adds detail but cannot stand alone.
Under the old bridge is a phrase. The river rushed is a clause because river is the subject and rushed is the verb.
Does it have both a subject and a verb?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: Under the old bridge is a phrase. The river rushed is a clause because river is the subject and rushed is the verb.
visual.check: Does it have both a subject and a verb?

script: In this submodule we learn Clauses And Phrases. A clause has a subject and verb. A phrase adds detail but cannot stand alone. First, watch the main pattern. Under the old bridge is a phrase. The river rushed is a clause because river is the subject and rushed is the verb. Now slow down and ask what job each word group is doing. Strong grammar is not about naming things for fun. It is about seeing how the sentence is built, so your own writing becomes clearer. Here is the check: Does it have both a subject and a verb? If you can explain why the answer works, you are ready to move to the next board. Practice pass: say the example aloud, then point to the exact word or word group doing the job. If the answer feels uncertain, use a two question test. First, what is the main subject and main verb? Second, is this extra word group naming, describing, connecting, or adding a condition? That two question test stops guesses. Let us do one more miniature example. In the sentence, While the lights flickered, the class kept reading, the main idea is the class kept reading. The opening clause tells when it happened. A confident writer can move that clause, shorten it, or keep it for rhythm. Your aim is not to memorize a label; your aim is to control meaning. When you can explain the job, you can use the pattern in your own writing.

title: Independent And Dependent Clauses
point: Independent clauses can stand alone. Dependent clauses need help.
captions: Independent clauses can stand alone. Dependent clauses need help.
The team cheered is complete. Because the team cheered makes the reader wait for another idea.
Can this group of words stand alone as a full thought?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: The team cheered is complete. Because the team cheered makes the reader wait for another idea.
visual.check: Can this group of words stand alone as a full thought?

script: In this submodule we learn Independent And Dependent Clauses. Independent clauses can stand alone. Dependent clauses need help. First, watch the main pattern. The team cheered is complete. Because the team cheered makes the reader wait for another idea. Now slow down and ask what job each word group is doing. Strong grammar is not about naming things for fun. It is about seeing how the sentence is built, so your own writing becomes clearer. Here is the check: Can this group of words stand alone as a full thought? If you can explain why the answer works, you are ready to move to the next board. Practice pass: say the example aloud, then point to the exact word or word group doing the job. If the answer feels uncertain, use a two question test. First, what is the main subject and main verb? Second, is this extra word group naming, describing, connecting, or adding a condition? That two question test stops guesses. Let us do one more miniature example. In the sentence, While the lights flickered, the class kept reading, the main idea is the class kept reading. The opening clause tells when it happened. A confident writer can move that clause, shorten it, or keep it for rhythm. Your aim is not to memorize a label; your aim is to control meaning. When you can explain the job, you can use the pattern in your own writing.

title: Relative Clauses
point: Relative clauses add detail about a noun using words like who, which, or that.
captions: Relative clauses add detail about a noun using words like who, which, or that.
The book that Mia borrowed was exciting. That Mia borrowed tells us which book.
Which noun is the relative clause describing?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: The book that Mia borrowed was exciting. That Mia borrowed tells us which book.
visual.check: Which noun is the relative clause describing?

script: In this submodule we learn Relative Clauses. Relative clauses add detail about a noun using words like who, which, or that. First, watch the main pattern. The book that Mia borrowed was exciting. That Mia borrowed tells us which book. Now slow down and ask what job each word group is doing. Strong grammar is not about naming things for fun. It is about seeing how the sentence is built, so your own writing becomes clearer. Here is the check: Which noun is the relative clause describing? If you can explain why the answer works, you are ready to move to the next board. Practice pass: say the example aloud, then point to the exact word or word group doing the job. If the answer feels uncertain, use a two question test. First, what is the main subject and main verb? Second, is this extra word group naming, describing, connecting, or adding a condition? That two question test stops guesses. Let us do one more miniature example. In the sentence, While the lights flickered, the class kept reading, the main idea is the class kept reading. The opening clause tells when it happened. A confident writer can move that clause, shorten it, or keep it for rhythm. Your aim is not to memorize a label; your aim is to control meaning. When you can explain the job, you can use the pattern in your own writing.

title: Compound And Complex Sentences
point: Compound sentences join equal ideas. Complex sentences add a dependent idea.
captions: Compound sentences join equal ideas. Complex sentences add a dependent idea.
I packed my bag, and I checked the map is compound. After I checked the map, I packed my bag is complex.
Are the ideas equal, or is one idea depending on another?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: I packed my bag, and I checked the map is compound. After I checked the map, I packed my bag is complex.
visual.check: Are the ideas equal, or is one idea depending on another?

script: In this submodule we learn Compound And Complex Sentences. Compound sentences join equal ideas. Complex sentences add a dependent idea. First, watch the main pattern. I packed my bag, and I checked the map is compound. After I checked the map, I packed my bag is complex. Now slow down and ask what job each word group is doing. Strong grammar is not about naming things for fun. It is about seeing how the sentence is built, so your own writing becomes clearer. Here is the check: Are the ideas equal, or is one idea depending on another? If you can explain why the answer works, you are ready to move to the next board. Practice pass: say the example aloud, then point to the exact word or word group doing the job. If the answer feels uncertain, use a two question test. First, what is the main subject and main verb? Second, is this extra word group naming, describing, connecting, or adding a condition? That two question test stops guesses. Let us do one more miniature example. In the sentence, While the lights flickered, the class kept reading, the main idea is the class kept reading. The opening clause tells when it happened. A confident writer can move that clause, shorten it, or keep it for rhythm. Your aim is not to memorize a label; your aim is to control meaning. When you can explain the job, you can use the pattern in your own writing.

title: Commas With Clauses
point: Commas help readers see where clause parts meet.
captions: Commas help readers see where clause parts meet.
Because the path was muddy, we wore boots. The comma marks the end of the opening dependent clause.
Does the sentence begin with a dependent clause?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: Because the path was muddy, we wore boots. The comma marks the end of the opening dependent clause.
visual.check: Does the sentence begin with a dependent clause?

script: In this submodule we learn Commas With Clauses. Commas help readers see where clause parts meet. First, watch the main pattern. Because the path was muddy, we wore boots. The comma marks the end of the opening dependent clause. Now slow down and ask what job each word group is doing. Strong grammar is not about naming things for fun. It is about seeing how the sentence is built, so your own writing becomes clearer. Here is the check: Does the sentence begin with a dependent clause? If you can explain why the answer works, you are ready to move to the next board. Practice pass: say the example aloud, then point to the exact word or word group doing the job. If the answer feels uncertain, use a two question test. First, what is the main subject and main verb? Second, is this extra word group naming, describing, connecting, or adding a condition? That two question test stops guesses. Let us do one more miniature example. In the sentence, While the lights flickered, the class kept reading, the main idea is the class kept reading. The opening clause tells when it happened. A confident writer can move that clause, shorten it, or keep it for rhythm. Your aim is not to memorize a label; your aim is to control meaning. When you can explain the job, you can use the pattern in your own writing.

title: Subject Verb Agreement
point: The verb must match the subject, even when extra words sit between them.
captions: The verb must match the subject, even when extra words sit between them.
The box of pencils is on the desk. Box is singular, so the verb is is.
Find the true subject before choosing the verb.
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: The box of pencils is on the desk. Box is singular, so the verb is is.
visual.check: Find the true subject before choosing the verb.

script: In this submodule we learn Subject Verb Agreement. The verb must match the subject, even when extra words sit between them. First, watch the main pattern. The box of pencils is on the desk. Box is singular, so the verb is is. Now slow down and ask what job each word group is doing. Strong grammar is not about naming things for fun. If you can explain why the answer works, you are ready to move to the next board. Practice pass: say the example aloud, then point to the exact word or word group doing the job. If the answer feels uncertain, use a two question test. First, what is the main subject and main verb? Second, is this extra word group naming, describing, connecting, or adding a condition? That two question test stops guesses. Let us do one more miniature example. In the sentence, While the lights flickered, the class kept reading, the main idea is the class kept reading. When you can explain the job, you can use the pattern in your own writing.

title: Pronoun Antecedents
point: A pronoun must clearly point back to its noun.
captions: A pronoun must clearly point back to its noun.
When Ava met Priya, she smiled can be unclear. The reader may not know who she means.
Can the reader tell exactly who the pronoun means?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: When Ava met Priya, she smiled can be unclear. The reader may not know who she means.
visual.check: Can the reader tell exactly who the pronoun means?

script: In this submodule we learn Pronoun Antecedents. A pronoun must clearly point back to its noun. First, watch the main pattern. When Ava met Priya, she smiled can be unclear. The reader may not know who she means. Now slow down and ask what job each word group is doing. Strong grammar is not about naming things for fun. It is about seeing how the sentence is built, so your own writing becomes clearer. Here is the check: Can the reader tell exactly who the pronoun means? If you can explain why the answer works, you are ready to move to the next board. Practice pass: say the example aloud, then point to the exact word or word group doing the job. If the answer feels uncertain, use a two question test. First, what is the main subject and main verb? Second, is this extra word group naming, describing, connecting, or adding a condition? That two question test stops guesses. Let us do one more miniature example. In the sentence, While the lights flickered, the class kept reading, the main idea is the class kept reading. The opening clause tells when it happened. A confident writer can move that clause, shorten it, or keep it for rhythm. Your aim is not to memorize a label; your aim is to control meaning. When you can explain the job, you can use the pattern in your own writing.

title: Misplaced Modifiers
point: A modifier should sit close to the word it describes.
captions: A modifier should sit close to the word it describes.
Running down the path, Sam saw the bus is clear. Sam is running.
Is the describing phrase next to the right noun?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: Running down the path, Sam saw the bus is clear. Sam is running.
visual.check: Is the describing phrase next to the right noun?

script: In this submodule we learn Misplaced Modifiers. A modifier should sit close to the word it describes. First, watch the main pattern. Running down the path, Sam saw the bus is clear. title: Parallel Structure
point: Parallel structure keeps items in a list in the same grammatical shape.
captions: Parallel structure keeps items in a list in the same grammatical shape.
Mia likes swimming, reading, and drawing. Each item is an ing word.
Do the listed items use the same pattern?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: Mia likes swimming, reading, and drawing. Each item is an ing word.
visual.check: Do the listed items use the same pattern?

script: In this submodule we learn Parallel Structure. Parallel structure keeps items in a list in the same grammatical shape. First, watch the main pattern. Mia likes swimming, reading, and drawing. title: Gerunds
point: A gerund is an ing verb form acting like a noun.
captions: A gerund is an ing verb form acting like a noun.
Swimming is fun. Swimming is the subject, so it is acting like a noun.
Is the ing word naming an activity or acting as the main verb?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: Swimming is fun. Swimming is the subject, so it is acting like a noun.
visual.check: Is the ing word naming an activity or acting as the main verb?

title: Participles
point: A participle is a verb form acting like an adjective.
captions: A participle is a verb form acting like an adjective.
The barking dog woke us. Barking describes dog, while woke is the main verb.
Which word is being described?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: The barking dog woke us. Barking describes dog, while woke is the main verb.

title: Infinitives
point: An infinitive uses to plus a verb and can act as a noun, adjective, or adverb.
captions: An infinitive uses to plus a verb and can act as a noun, adjective, or adverb.
To win requires practice. To win is the thing being discussed.
What job is the to phrase doing?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: To win requires practice. To win is the thing being discussed.
visual.check: What job is the to phrase doing?

title: Active And Passive Voice
point: Active voice puts the doer first. Passive voice puts the receiver first.
captions: Active voice puts the doer first. Passive voice puts the receiver first.
The student solved the puzzle is active. The puzzle was solved by the student is passive.
Who is doing the action?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: The student solved the puzzle is active. The puzzle was solved by the student is passive.

script: In this submodule we learn Active And Passive Voice. Active voice puts the doer first. Passive voice puts the receiver first. First, watch the main pattern. The student solved the puzzle is active. The puzzle was solved by the student is passive. Now slow down and ask what job each word group is doing. Strong grammar is not about naming things for fun. It is about seeing how the sentence is built, so your own writing becomes clearer. Here is the check: Who is doing the action? If you can explain why the answer works, you are ready to move to the next board. Practice pass: say the example aloud, then point to the exact word or word group doing the job. If the answer feels uncertain, use a two question test. First, what is the main subject and main verb? Second, is this extra word group naming, describing, connecting, or adding a condition? That two question test stops guesses. Let us do one more miniature example. In the sentence, While the lights flickered, the class kept reading, the main idea is the class kept reading. The opening clause tells when it happened. A confident writer can move that clause, shorten it, or keep it for rhythm. Your aim is not to memorize a label; your aim is to control meaning. When you can explain the job, you can use the pattern in your own writing.

title: Subjunctive Mood
point: The subjunctive handles wishes, demands, and unreal situations.
captions: The subjunctive handles wishes, demands, and unreal situations.
If I were captain, I would choose a calm plan. Were signals an unreal situation.
Is the sentence real, or imagined?
Use the pattern in your own sentence.

visual.example: If I were captain, I would choose a calm plan. Were signals an unreal situation.
visual.check: Is the sentence real, or imagined?

title: Appositives
point: An appositive renames a noun right beside it.
captions: An appositive renames a noun right beside it.

title: Ellipsis And Omission
point: Sometimes words are left out because the pattern makes them understood.
captions: Sometimes words are left out because the pattern makes them understood.
Ava chose blue; Omar, green. The second clause leaves out chose.

Use the pattern in your own sentence.

title: Sentence Variety
point: Mature writing mixes short, compound, complex, and opening-phrase sentences.
captions: Mature writing mixes short, compound, complex, and opening-phrase sentences.

title: High Level Grammar Editing
point: Advanced editing checks clarity, emphasis, rhythm, and precision.
captions: Advanced editing checks clarity, emphasis, rhythm, and precision.
